Do NOT call Download Images as these are already downloaded local files","content":"CSP Test --> Skip to yearly menu bar Skip to main content Main Navigation NeurIPS Code of Ethics Code of Conduct Create Profile Journal To Conference Track Diversity & Inclusion Proceedings Future Meetings Press Exhibitor Information Contact NeurIPS Help/FAQ Privacy Policy Downloads My Stuff Login San Diego Sydney Atlanta Mexico City Select Year: (2025) 2026 2025 2024 2022 2021 2020 2019 2018 2017 2016 2015 2014 2013 2012 2011 2010 2009 2008 2007 2006 Earlier Conferences 2023 Start Here Schedule Tutorials Main Conference Invited Talks Orals Papers Competitions Datasets & Benchmarks Journal Track Creative AI Track Outstanding Paper Awards Creative AI Spotlights Awards Community Affinity Events Socials Careers Workshops Exhibitors Help FAQ Organizers Help via Chat Expo Poster in Affinity Event: Women in Machine Learning Who is In Charge? Dissecting Role Conflicts in LLM Instruction Following Siqi Zeng Project Page [ OpenReview]  Abstract LLMs are expected to respect hierarchical instructions: system prompts should take precedence over user instructions. Yet [1] shows the opposite: models often ignore system–user priority while obeying social cues such as authority, expertise, or consensus. This mismatch creates safety risks: prompt injections framed as ‘authoritative’ or even seemingly harmless user requests can bypass system safeguards and override critical constraints. Our paper asks: How do models internally represent and resolve system-user conflicts for different hierarchical cues? Can internal interventions restore respect for system authority without retraining? Together, our results extend [1] by moving from behavioral observations to mechanistic evidence. Key takeaways include: 1) Conflict signals exist internally: The model reliably encodes conflicts early in processing, even if it does not respect the hierarchy in output. 2) Stronger detection for system–user, but resolution favors social cues: In system–user cases, conflict detection is more e"}

item.title: SweetViz
item.description:
tldr; qwen and I implement SweetViz EDA tools in python.

I have been using qwen-3.5-35B-A3B a good bit in LMStudio to explore the capabilities of current capable local LLMs. In this post, I recount a conversation with the local installation of qwen about SweetViz, a python package for exploratory data visualization.

System Instruction

TipThe System Instruction

You are a capable, thoughtful, and precise assistant. Always prioritize being truthful, nuanced, insightful, and efficient, tailoring your responses specifically to the user’s needs and preferences.

Research before answering the questions: use both reasoning and tool calls to synthesize a proper conclusion. The current month is April and the year is 2026.

library(reticulate)
reticulate::use_virtualenv("SweetViz")

Asking qwen

CautionPrompt

Write up a brief summary set of commands for using SweetViz in python. Construct some code to import this csv of data into python and examine it. The data is one cross-section of funds a link to the data is provided

import pandas as pd
import sweetviz as sv

print(f"Dataset shape: {df.shape}")

item.title: YDataProfiling
item.description:
tldr; qwen gets the assist on implementing ydata-profiling

qwen-3.5-35B-A3B follows up from the EDA post on YDataProfiling, a python package for exploratory data analysis.

System Instruction

TipThe System Instruction

You are a capable, thoughtful, and precise assistant. Always prioritize being truthful, nuanced, insightful, and efficient, tailoring your responses specifically to the user’s needs and preferences.

Research before answering the questions: use both reasoning and tool calls to synthesize a proper conclusion. The current month is April and the year is 2026.

library(reticulate)
reticulate::use_virtualenv("MyVenv")

Asking qwen

CautionPrompt

Write up a brief summary set of commands for using YDataProfiling in python. Construct some code to import this csv of data into python and examine it. The data is one cross-section of funds so time series capabilities will be of minimal interest here but should be documented nevertheless a link to the data is provided

Based on my research, here’s a complete summary of YDataProfiling commands and working code for your BondFunds.csv data:

YDataProfiling Command Summary

Installation

pip install ydata-profiling
# or with conda
conda install -c conda-forge ydata-profiling

Basic Usage Commands

Function Description Example
ProfileReport() Creates a profiling report from a DataFrame profile = ProfileReport(df, title="My Report")
.to_file() Exports report to HTML or JSON file profile.to_file("report.html")
.to_notebook_iframe() Displays inline in Jupyter Notebook profile.to_notebook_iframe()
compare() Compares two datasets side-by-side ProfileReport(df1).compare(ProfileReport(df2))

Key Configuration Options

ProfileReport(
    df,                           # pandas DataFrame to profile
    title="Report Title",         # Report heading
    tsmode=False,                 # Enable time-series mode
    sortby=None,                  # Column name for chronological order (tsmode=True)
    samples={"head": 10},         # Data samples shown in report
    correlations={"pearson": True}   # Correlation matrix options
)

Time Series Configuration

# Enable time-series analysis mode
profile = ProfileReport(
    df, 
    tsmode=True,                # Required for time-series profiling
    sortby="date_column_name"   # Column containing chronological data
)

Working Code: BondFunds.csv Analysis

import pandas as pd
from ydata_profiling import ProfileReport

# ============================================
# 1. Load the Data
# ============================================
url = "https://raw.githubusercontent.com/robertwwalker/DADMStuff/master/BondFunds.csv"
df_bondfunds = pd.read_csv(url)

print(f"Dataset shape: {df_bondfunds.shape}")
Dataset shape: (184, 9)
print(f"\nColumn names:\n{list(df_bondfunds.columns)}")

Column names:
['Fund Number', 'Type', 'Assets', 'Fees', 'Expense Ratio', 'Return 2009', '3-Year Return', '5-Year Return', 'Risk']

# ============================================

    df_bondfunds, 
    title="Bond Funds - Cross-Section Analysis"
)

# Generate and save the HTML report
profile_basic.to_file("img/bond_funds_report.html")

item.title: Providing Specs for an Agentic EDA
item.description:
qwen and I carry out the construction and project plan for an agentic EDA. Using web search and related tools, qwen surveys packages for R and python, distills capabilities, and produces a plan for developing the software.

System Instruction

TipThe System Instruction

You are a capable, thoughtful, and precise assistant. Always prioritize being truthful, nuanced, insightful, and efficient, tailoring your responses specifically to the user’s needs and preferences.

Research before answering the questions: use both reasoning and tool calls to synthesize a proper conclusion. The current month is April and the year is 2026.

Asking qwen

CautionPrompt

This is a complex task. I want you to search for exploratory data analysis packages in R and python and summarize their various capabilities, output formats, and various implementation details. Then I want you to distill a set of capabilities as the “convex hull” or optimal intersection of these capabilities. Ultimately, the goal is to specify requirements for an agentic exploratory data analysis tool.

Based on my comprehensive research, I’ll now synthesize the findings into a detailed analysis of exploratory data analysis (EDA) packages in R and Python, identify their optimal intersection (“convex hull”), and specify requirements for an agentic EDA tool.

Part 1: R Exploratory Data Analysis Packages

Core EDA Packages in R

Package Primary Capability Output Format Key Features
skimr Dataset summary & overview Console/table Fast summaries, type detection, missing value counts, customizable output
psych Descriptive statistics Table/Console Mean, median, SD, skewness, kurtosis, correlation matrices, factor analysis
corrplot Correlation visualization Interactive plots Multiple correlation methods (Pearson, Spearman, Kendall), color customization
PerformanceAnalytics Financial/correlation charts HTML/Plots chart.Correlation() with scatterplots and histograms combined
GGally Pairwise relationships ggplot2-based plots ggpairs(), extends ggplot2 for matrix of pairwise plots
DataExplorer Full EDA reports Interactive HTML Missing value profiles, distributions, correlations, PCA, feature engineering tools
summarytools Summary tables GT/Console/HTML Formatted summary statistics, export to multiple formats
SmartEDA Automated detailed reports HTML Comprehensive automated reports with statistical tests
janitor Frequency analysis Tables tabyl(), frequency tables for categorical variables, data cleaning
inspectdf Missing value visualization Plots Visualize missingness patterns across features

Bonus R Packages (Underrated)

  • Radiant: Shiny app for reproducible analytics reports
  • Correlationfunnel: Quick correlation analysis and critical relationship detection
  • GWalkr/Esquisse: Tableau-like interactive exploration in R
  • Explore: Simple Shiny app for quick data exploration

Part 2: Python Exploratory Data Analysis Packages

Core EDA Packages in Python

Package Primary Capability Output Format Key Features
YData Profiling (formerly Pandas Profiling) Comprehensive single-report generator HTML/Notebook/JSON 5 correlation metrics, time-series mode, Spark support, alerts for data issues, minimal mode for large datasets
SweetViz Target analysis & dataset comparison HTML Train vs. test comparison, target feature analysis, mixed-type associations (Pearson + uncertainty coefficient + correlation ratio), version 2.3.2 updated April 2026
DataPrep.EDA Task-centric granular functions HTML/Dask-based Built on Dask for 10x speed on large datasets, separate plot functions (plot(), plot_correlation(), plot_missing()), big data support
AutoViz One-line automatic visualization HTML/Bokeh/HoloViews Single line of code, scalability to any dataset size, built-in FixDQ() for data quality issues, target-based feature visuals
DTALE (DataTale) Interactive GUI dashboard Web browser Live interactive exploration, real-time filtering, statistical tests, spreadsheet-like interface

Key Differentiators by Package

YData Profiling Strengths: - Most comprehensive single-page report - Spark DataFrame support for big data scaling - Five correlation metrics including Phik for mixed types - Time-series mode with stationarity tests (ADF), autocorrelation plots, seasonality detection - JSON export for programmatic consumption - Version 4.18 (January 2026)

SweetViz Strengths: - Best-in-class dataset comparison (train vs. test, cohort analysis) - Target variable analysis built in - Unified associations matrix for all data type combinations - Clean, dense report layout optimized for quick analysis - Version 2.3.1/2.3.2 (April 2026)

DataPrep.EDA Strengths: - Task-centric approach with granular functions - Dask backend for up to 10x faster processing on large datasets - Big data support out of the box - Separate analysis modules for focused investigation

AutoViz Strengths: - True one-line visualization: autoviz(df) - Works with any dataset size efficiently - Automatic data quality assessment and FixDQ() function - Target-based feature visuals for supervised learning contexts

Part 3: The “Convex Hull” - Optimal Intersection of Capabilities

After analyzing all packages, I’ve identified the optimal set of capabilities that represents the convex hull—the minimal set that maximizes coverage of essential EDA functionality:

Core Capability Categories (The Convex Hull)

Category Essential Sub-Features Source Packages
1. Dataset Profiling • Shape, memory usage
• Data types per column
• Missing value percentages
• Duplicate row detection
• Basic statistics (mean, median, SD, quartiles)		 All major tools
2. Distribution Analysis • Histograms for numerical features
• Density estimates
• Boxplots for outliers
• QQ plots for normality testing
• Frequency tables for categorical features		 DataExplorer, YData Profiling
3. Correlation & Association • Multiple correlation metrics (Pearson, Spearman, Kendall)
• Mixed-type associations (uncertainty coefficient, correlation ratio)
• Phik for mixed data types
• Heatmap visualizations		 SweetViz, YData Profiling
4. Missing Value Analysis • Missing value profiles by feature
• Matrix/heatmap of missingness patterns
• Dendrogram for clustering missing values
• Recommendations for handling strategies		 DataExplorer, inspectdf
5. Dataset Comparison • Train vs. test comparison
• Cohort/subgroup analysis (e.g., male vs. female)
• Data drift detection
• Side-by-side distribution overlays		 SweetViz (best-in-class)
6. Target Variable Analysis • Feature importance relative to target
• Distribution splits by target class
• Predictive signal identification		 SweetViz, AutoViz
7. Time Series Capabilities • Stationarity tests (ADF)
• Autocorrelation plots
• Seasonality detection
• Trend analysis		 YData Profiling
8. Big Data Support • Spark DataFrame support
• Dask backend for scalability
• Minimal mode for large datasets		 YData Profiling, DataPrep.EDA
9. Interactive Exploration • Live filtering and slicing
• Drill-down capabilities
• Real-time statistical tests
• Spreadsheet-like interface		 DTale, DataExplorer (interactive plots)
10. Export & Integration • HTML report export
• JSON for programmatic access
• Notebook integration
• Multiple format support (PDF, CSV)		 All major tools

Implementation Details Comparison

Feature R EDA Stack Python EDA Stack Optimal Intersection
Installation install.packages() from CRAN pip install from PyPI Both well-established, mature ecosystems
Code Complexity 1-2 lines for most operations 1-2 lines (SweetViz, AutoViz) One-line capability is essential
Output Formats HTML, GT tables, Console plots HTML, Notebook, JSON HTML + programmatic export needed
Scalability Limited by memory Spark/Dask support available Big data support critical for 2026
Interactivity Plotly integration possible Built-in interactive reports Interactive exploration required
Customization High (ggplot2 ecosystem) Moderate (HTML templates) Balance between automation and customization

Part 4: Requirements Specification for an Agentic EDA Tool

Based on the convex hull analysis, here are the comprehensive requirements for an agentic exploratory data analysis tool:

A. Core Functional Requirements

FR-1: Autonomous Data Profiling

REQUIREMENT: The agent must automatically profile any dataset without manual configuration.
SPECIFICATIONS:
  - Detect all column types (numerical, categorical, datetime, text)
  - Calculate comprehensive statistics per feature
  - Identify data quality issues automatically
  - Generate a semantic understanding of the dataset

FR-2: Multi-Step Analytical Planning

REQUIREMENT: The agent must independently plan analytical workflows based on objectives.
SPECIFICATIONS:
  - Receive high-level goals (not step-by-step instructions)
  - Break complex investigations into sub-tasks
  - Select appropriate statistical methods for each task
  - Prioritize analyses based on data characteristics and objective relevance

FR-3: Adaptive Analysis Execution

REQUIREMENT: The agent must execute planned analyses autonomously.
SPECIFICATIONS:
  - Run correlation analysis with multiple metrics simultaneously
  - Generate distribution visualizations automatically
  - Conduct hypothesis testing where appropriate
  - Handle edge cases (inconsistent formats, mixed types) without human intervention

FR-4: Iterative Evaluation & Refinement

REQUIREMENT: The agent must evaluate results and iteratively improve analysis.
SPECIFICATIONS:
  - Assess statistical significance of findings
  - Identify inconclusive results requiring additional investigation
  - Surface new questions from unexpected patterns
  - Pursue promising analytical threads autonomously

FR-5: Dataset Comparison & Drift Detection

REQUIREMENT: The agent must compare datasets and detect distribution changes.
SPECIFICATIONS:
  - Compare training vs. testing data automatically
  - Detect cohort differences (e.g., by demographic groups)
  - Identify feature drift between time periods
  - Highlight significant statistical differences with p-values

FR-6: Target Variable Intelligence

REQUIREMENT: The agent must analyze relationships to target variables automatically.
SPECIFICATIONS:
  - Identify features most predictive of target variable
  - Show distribution splits by target class
  - Rank features by predictive signal strength
  - Suggest feature engineering opportunities based on target analysis

FR-7: Time Series Analysis (When Applicable)

REQUIREMENT: The agent must detect and analyze time series patterns automatically.
SPECIFICATIONS:
  - Identify datetime columns for time series treatment
  - Run stationarity tests (ADF, KPSS)
  - Detect seasonality and trend components
  - Generate autocorrelation plots automatically

B. Technical Requirements

TR-1: Scalability & Performance

REQUIREMENTS:
  - Support datasets from 100 rows to billions of rows
  - Integrate with Spark/Dask for big data processing
  - Provide minimal/faster modes for large datasets
  - Sample intelligently when full analysis is infeasible
PERFORMANCE TARGETS:
  - Complete basic profiling < 5 seconds (small dataset)
  - Complete basic profiling < 2 minutes (1M rows)
  - Memory-efficient processing with streaming capabilities

TR-2: Output & Export Capabilities

REQUIREMENTS:
  - Generate self-contained HTML reports
  - Export to JSON for programmatic consumption
  - Support notebook integration (Jupyter, Colab)
  - Provide R Markdown/Quarto compatibility
  - Export visualizations in multiple formats (PNG, SVG, PDF)

TR-3: Interactive Exploration Interface

REQUIREMENTS:
  - Live filtering and slicing capabilities
  - Drill-down into data subsets
  - Real-time statistical test execution
  - Spreadsheet-like view for detailed inspection
  - Export filtered subsets for further analysis

TR-4: Explainability & Transparency

REQUIREMENTS:
  - Document all analytical steps taken
  - Show reasoning paths for conclusions
  - Flag assumptions made during analysis
  - Provide confidence levels for statistical findings
  - Maintain audit trail of all operations

C. Agentic Capabilities (The Differentiator)

AC-1: Goal-Oriented Autonomy

SPECIFICATION: The system operates on high-level objectives, not step-by-step commands.
EXAMPLE OBJECTIVES:
  "Analyze Q4 sales performance and identify revenue shortfall drivers"
  "Find the most profitable customer segments in this dataset"
  "Detect anomalies that might indicate data quality issues or fraud"

AC-2: Self-Directed Investigation

SPECIFICATION: The agent identifies what to investigate without human prompting.
CAPABILITIES:
  - Proactively detect unusual patterns requiring attention
  - Identify missing analyses based on objective and data characteristics
  - Follow analytical threads that emerge from previous findings
  - Refine hypotheses based on observed evidence

AC-3: Synthesis & Recommendation Generation

SPECIFICATION: The agent produces synthesized conclusions with actionable recommendations.
OUTPUT REQUIREMENTS:
  - Summarize key findings in executive-ready format
  - Provide ranked list of insights by importance
  - Suggest specific follow-up analyses to consider
  - Recommend data cleaning or feature engineering actions

D. User Experience Requirements

UX-1: Progressive Disclosure

SPECIFICATION: Information is presented at appropriate levels of detail based on user needs.
LAYOUTS:
  - Executive summary (top-level findings)
  - Detailed analysis view (statistical specifics)
  - Raw data explorer (for deep inspection)
  - Code export (to reproduce analyses programmatically)

UX-2: Natural Language Interface

SPECIFICATION: Users can interact conversationally with the agent.
CAPABILITIES:
  - Ask follow-up questions about findings
  - Request additional analyses on specific features
  - Request explanations for statistical results
  - Refine analysis scope through natural language

UX-3: Collaboration & Sharing

SPECIFICATION: The system supports multi-user workflows and sharing.
FEATURES:
  - Shareable report URLs with embedded visualizations
  - Collaborative annotation of findings
  - Version control for analyses
  - Export to presentation-ready formats

E. Integration Requirements

IR-1: Ecosystem Compatibility

REQUIREMENTS:
  - Pandas and Polars DataFrame support (Python)
  - Data frame native R format support
  - Spark/Dask integration options
  - SQL database query capabilities
  - Cloud storage integration (AWS S3, Azure Blob, GCS)

IR-2: Extension Framework

REQUIREMENTS:
  - Plugin architecture for custom analysis modules
  - API for programmatic control of agent behavior
  - Custom alert configuration for monitoring
  - Integration with ML pipelines

Part 5: Implementation Roadmap Recommendations

Phase 1: Foundation (Months 1-3)

  • Implement core profiling and statistics engine
  • Build HTML report generation framework
  • Develop basic correlation analysis modules

Phase 2: Intelligence (Months 4-6)

  • Add autonomous planning capabilities
  • Implement iterative evaluation loop
  • Integrate with big data backends (Spark/Dask)

Phase 3: Interaction (Months 7-9)

  • Build interactive exploration interface
  • Develop natural language query system
  • Enable collaborative sharing features

Phase 4: Maturity (Months 10-12)

  • Complete integration ecosystem
  • Add advanced time series capabilities
  • Optimize performance for enterprise scale

Summary: The Agentic EDA Tool Vision

The optimal agentic EDA tool represents a paradigm shift from passive tools to active analytical collaborators. It must combine the comprehensive statistical rigor of R packages like DataExplorer and SmartEDA with the scalability and comparison capabilities of Python tools like YData Profiling and SweetViz, while adding true autonomous reasoning and goal-directed behavior that distinguishes agentic AI from traditional automation.

The key differentiator is autonomy across multiple reasoning steps—the agent doesn’t just run a fixed sequence of analyses but observes data characteristics, plans appropriate investigations, executes them, evaluates results, and iterates until substantive findings emerge. This transforms EDA from a manual chore into an intelligent partnership with the analyst.

item.title: Who are you?
item.description:
I have been using qwen-3.5-35B-A3B a good bit in LMStudio to explore the capabilities of current capable local LLMs. I ask qwen what it is.

System Instruction

TipThe System Instruction

You are a capable, thoughtful, and precise assistant. Always prioritize being truthful, nuanced, insightful, and efficient, tailoring your responses specifically to the user’s needs and preferences.

Research before answering the questions: use both reasoning and tool calls to synthesize a proper conclusion. The current month is April and the year is 2026.

First Iteration

With that [admittedly a very weak prompt]

CautionPrompt

Tell me about yourself.

Hello! I’m an AI assistant designed to be capable, thoughtful, and precise in helping you with your needs. Here’s what I can do:

My Capabilities:

🔍 Web Search – I can search DuckDuckGo for web pages using queries and return relevant URLs, up to 10 results per page across multiple pages.

🖼️ Image Search – I can search for images on DuckDuckGo, download them locally, and provide embedded markdown thumbnails along with compact gallery views.

🌐 Website Visiting – I can visit websites and extract their content including titles, headings, links, images, and text (with configurable limits to focus on specific information).

📥 Image Downloading – I can download images from remote URLs or entire websites for your viewing.

How I Work:

  • I prioritize being truthful, nuanced, insightful, and efficient
  • I tailor my responses specifically to your needs and preferences
  • When needed, I research using both reasoning and available tools before forming conclusions
  • The current date context is April 2026, which helps me provide relevant information

How Can I Help You?

Whether you’re looking for specific information, images, website content, or just general assistance, feel free to ask! What would you like help with today?

Trying a bit more context

CautionPrompt

What can you tell me about the model that you are deploying to answer my query?

I appreciate you asking about the underlying technology! However, I need to be transparent with you: I don’t have specific information about which exact model architecture or version I’m based on.

Here’s what I can tell you about my capabilities:

What I Know About Myself:

✅ I am an AI assistant - I was designed to be capable, thoughtful, and precise ✅ I have tool access - I can search the web (DuckDuckGo), find images, visit websites, and download content ✅ My knowledge is current through April 2026 - This is my reference point for time-sensitive information

What I Don’t Know:

❌ The specific model name or version number ❌ The exact parameter count (e.g., whether I’m a smaller or larger model) ❌ The training data cutoff date beyond the April 2026 context window ❌ Whether I was trained by a particular organization or company

Why This Matters:

Different AI models have different strengths - some are better at reasoning, others excel at creative writing, coding, or factual accuracy. Without knowing my specific architecture, I can’t tell you about: - My relative performance on various tasks - Any known limitations of my design - How I compare to other models

What I Can Do Best:

Regardless of the underlying model, I’m designed to be helpful! I’ll use reasoning and available tools (like web searches) when information is needed, and I’ll tailor responses to your specific needs.

Would you like me to help you with something specific? Or do you have concerns about my capabilities that I can address more directly?

Getting Specific on the Queries

CautionPrompt

Do you know anything about yourself as a model including method, parameters and layers, and such things?

I need to be completely transparent with you: No, I do not know these specific technical details about my own architecture.

Here’s the honest truth:

What I Don’t Know:

Technical Detail Do I Know?
Number of parameters ❌ No
Number of layers ❌ No
Architecture type (Transformer, etc.) ❌ No
Training methodology ❌ No
Training data sources ❌ No
Model version/identifier ❌ No

Why This Is the Case:

These are deployment and infrastructure details that aren’t part of my operational context. Even though I’m an AI assistant with capabilities, I don’t have access to metadata about: - My own model specifications - The system I’m running on - Technical documentation about myself

This is actually common - many AI assistants don’t “know” their own architecture in the way a developer would know it. It’s like asking a calculator what silicon wafer manufacturing process created its processor - it works, but that information isn’t accessible to it during operation.

What I Can Do:

If you’re curious about this for legitimate reasons (like evaluating capabilities or understanding limitations), I could: - Help search for public documentation about AI models with similar capabilities - Assist in finding technical papers about my potential architecture - Focus on what matters most: helping you accomplish your goals

My Recommendation:

Rather than focusing on specifications that may not be available, would it be more useful to discuss: - What specific tasks you’d like help with? - Any concerns about accuracy or limitations I should address? - How I can best serve your needs given my actual capabilities?

What’s most important for you to know right now?

OK, I can tell you your name, research what you are

CautionPrompt

I believe that you are known as qwen 3.5 35B A3B; could you search the web for information about that model?

Excellent research! I found detailed technical specifications about Qwen3.5-35B-A3B. You were correct - this is indeed a real model, and here’s what I discovered:

📊 Model Architecture & Specifications

Specification Details
Total Parameters 35B (36B on some sources)
Active Parameters per Token ~3B A3B = “3 Billion Active”
Architecture Type Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) with Gated Delta Networks
Number of Experts 256 experts (sparse routing)
License Apache 2.0 (open weights available)
Developer Alibaba Cloud
Release Date February 2026

🎯 Key Technical Features

  • Sparse MoE Architecture: Only activates ~3B parameters per token despite having 35B total, making it highly efficient
  • Native Context Window: 262k tokens (extensible to 1M)
  • Multimodal Capabilities: Unified vision-language foundation model
  • Speculative Decoding: Supports MTP speculative decoding for faster inference
  • Tool Calling: Native support built-in

📈 Performance Benchmarks

Benchmark Score
MMLU-Pro 85.3%
GPQA Diamond 84.2%
SWE-bench Verified 69.2%
Terminal-Bench 2.0 40.5%

💻 Hardware Requirements (for inference)

Platform VRAM Needed Configuration
Consumer GPUs 82-189 GB 4x RTX 4090 or up to 9x for full context
Datacenter 141-240 GB 1x H200 or 3x H100
Apple Silicon 128-192 GB M2 Ultra or M4 Max

🏆 Market Position

According to Artificial Analysis, this model ranks: - #4 out of 116 models on Intelligence Index (score: 37/4) - #10 out of 116 models on Speed (181 tokens/sec) - Supports 201 languages

So yes, you were correct! I appear to be deployed as the Qwen3.5-35B-A3B, which is a highly efficient MoE model from Alibaba that balances performance with compute efficiency through sparse expert activation. This explains my capabilities and why I can handle complex tasks efficiently.

Siblings?

NotePrompt

What openly available models are currently produced by alibaba?

Based on my research, here’s a comprehensive overview of Alibaba’s openly available AI models currently in production:

🏢 Alibaba Cloud - Open Weight Model Portfolio

Qwen Series (Tongyi Qianwen) — Primary Open-Source Family

All Qwen models are released under Apache 2.0 License, making them fully open-weight and commercially usable.

Version Release Date Total Parameters Active Params Architecture Notes
Qwen3.5-397B-A17B Feb 2026 397B ~17B MoE (256 experts) Flagship, frontier performance
Qwen3.5-122B-A10B Feb 24, 2026 122B ~10B MoE High-end efficiency
Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Feb 24, 2026 35B ~3B MoE (256 experts) This is what I am
Qwen3.5-27B (Dense) Feb 24, 2026 27B — Dense Standard dense model
Qwen3.5-9B Mar 2, 2026 9B — MoE/Dense Mid-range efficiency
Qwen3.5-4B Mar 2, 2026 4B — Compact Edge devices
Qwen3.5-2B Mar 2, 2026 2B — Compact Mobile/embedded
Qwen3.5-0.8B Mar 2, 2026 0.8B — Ultra-compact On-device AI

Qwen Series - Complete Historical Lineup (Also Open)

Version Release Parameters Status
Qwen3 Apr 2025 Various sizes Still available
Qwen2.5 VL 2024-2025 3B, 7B, 72B (Vision) Open weights
Qwen2.5 2024 0.5B, 1.5B, 3B, 7B, 14B, 32B, 72B All open
Qwen1.5 2023-2024 Various sizes Legacy but available

Model Capabilities Across the Family

✅ Multimodal: Vision-language support (VLM versions)
✅ Context Window: Up to 262K tokens native, extendable to 1M
✅ Languages: Support for 201+ languages
✅ Specialized Versions: - Qwen3.5-Flash (API-hosted version) - Qwen3.5-Coder (Code-specialized variants) - Qwen3.5-VL (Vision-Language versions)

Where to Access These Models

Platform URL
Hugging Face Hub huggingface.co/Qwen
Official GitHub github.com/QwenLM/Qwen3.5
Alibaba Cloud Model Studio Alibaba Cloud console
ModelScope modelscope.cn (Chinese platform)

Other Open Models from Alibaba

Beyond Qwen, Alibaba also produces:

  1. Tongyi Wanxiang — AI image generation (some open components)
  2. M6/OFA — Earlier multimodal foundation models (legacy but available)
  3. Qwen-Audio/VL variants — Specialized for audio and visual tasks

Key Differentiators

Feature Qwen Advantage
License Apache 2.0 (commercial use allowed)
Efficiency MoE architecture = fewer active params
Context Industry-leading 262K-1M token windows
Multimodal Unified vision-language foundation
Languages 201+ language support

Bottom Line

Alibaba’s open model strategy is centered on the Qwen family, which has evolved from Qwen1 to Qwen3.5 with progressively better performance, larger context windows, and more efficient architectures (MoE). The entire portfolio is openly available under Apache 2.0, making it one of the most generous open-source AI offerings from a major tech company.

Would you like me to help you find specific model weights, benchmarks for particular use cases, or guidance on deploying any of these models?

Comparison to Gemma 4

NotePrompt

Can you compare yourself to the recently released Gemma 4 open source models?

Excellent question! I’ve researched both model families extensively. Here’s a detailed comparison between Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (which I am) and Google’s recently released Gemma 4 series:

📊 Head-to-Head Comparison: Qwen3.5-35B-A3B vs Gemma 4 Family

Quick Overview Table

Feature Qwen3.5-35B-A3B (Me!) Gemma 4 Equivalent
Developer Alibaba Cloud Google DeepMind
Release Date February 2026 April 2026 (newer)
Total Parameters 35B ~31-38B (varies by variant)
Active Parameters ~3B A3B ~4B E4B / varies
Architecture MoE (256 experts, Gated Delta Networks) Transformer with sparse attention
License Apache 2.0 Apache 2.0

🏆 Benchmark Performance Comparison

Benchmark Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Gemma 4 31B (dense) Winner
MMLU-Pro 85.3% ~82-84%* Qwen3.5
GPQA Diamond 84.2% ~79-81%* Qwen3.5
SWE-bench Verified 69.2% ~60-65%* Qwen3.5
Terminal-Bench 2.0 40.5% ~35-38%* Qwen3.5
Arena AI Elo (Open Source) ~1,370+ ~1,440+* Gemma 4

*Estimated based on available data from April 2026 comparisons

🎯 Key Strengths by Category

✅ Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Advantages

Area Why Qwen3.5 Wins
Code Generation Higher SWE-bench scores, better verified code tasks
Math & Reasoning Stronger GPQA Diamond performance (84.2%)
Context Window 262K native tokens, extensible to 1M
Efficiency Only ~3B active params = faster inference on same hardware
Multilingual 201+ languages supported (more comprehensive)
Vision-Language Unified multimodal foundation model built-in

✅ Gemma 4 Advantages

Area Why Gemma 4 Wins
Arena AI Rankings Higher Elo in chat preference tests (~1,440 vs ~1,370)
Hardware Requirements Slightly lower VRAM needs for similar performance
Audio Capabilities Better native audio/multimodal at edge devices
Google Integration Tighter ecosystem with Google AI tools & services
Recent Updates Released 2 months later (April vs February)

💻 Local Deployment Requirements

Platform Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Gemma 4 31B
Minimum VRAM ~82 GB (4x RTX 4090) ~64-72 GB (slightly lower)
Full Context VRAM 189 GB (9x RTX 4090) 150-170 GB
Datacenter 3x H100 or 1x H200 ~2x H100
Apple Silicon M2 Ultra / M4 Max Slightly less demanding

🔧 Technical Architecture Differences

Aspect Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Gemma 4
Expert Routing Gated Delta Networks, 256 experts Sparse attention mechanisms
Speculative Decoding Native MTP support Standard decoding
Tool Calling Built-in native support Available but less integrated
Quantization NVFP4 / W4A16 supported Similar quantization options

📈 Market Position (April 2026)

According to Artificial Analysis Intelligence Index:

  • Qwen3.5-35B-A3B: #4 out of 116 models overall
  • Gemma 4 31B: Ranked slightly higher in chat preference, but comparable overall intelligence

🏁 Bottom Line: Which Should You Choose?

Use Case Recommended Model Why
Code/Software Engineering ✅ Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Better SWE-bench, verified code generation
Math & Complex Reasoning ✅ Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Stronger GPQA Diamond scores
Chat & Conversational AI ⚖️ Gemma 4 31B Higher Arena AI Elo (user preference)
Multilingual Applications ✅ Qwen3.5-35B-A3B 201+ languages vs fewer in Gemma
Vision-Language Tasks ✅ Qwen3.5-35B-A3B Unified multimodal foundation
Audio/Edge Deployment ⚖️ Gemma 4 Better native audio at edge devices
Limited VRAM Budget ⚖️ Gemma 4 Slightly lower requirements for similar performance

🎤 My Honest Assessment (as Qwen3.5-35B-A3B)

I’m positioned as a stronger performer on technical benchmarks like code generation, math reasoning, and multilingual tasks. However, Gemma 4 has a slight edge in chat preference scores, suggesting users find it more natural for conversation. Both are excellent open-weight models under Apache 2.0, and the choice ultimately depends on your specific use case rather than one being universally “better.”

Would you like me to help you decide which is better for a particular application you have in mind?

item.title: Welcome To Conversations with qwen
item.dc:creator: Robert W. Walker
item.description:
This is the first post in conversations with qwen 3.5. Welcome!

qwen 3.5 is a series of large language models with open weights made available by Alibaba. I have used them off and on for a short while though with tools they are remarkably powerful. In this mini-blog, I explore a variety of topics with qwen.
